items.myth: Vectorization means NumPy is always faster than any loop.
items.reality: Vectorization wins on large numeric arrays where compiled ufuncs amortize overhead. Tiny arrays, heavy Python callbacks passed to `np.vectorize`, or algorithms with irregular control flow may still be faster or clearer with plain Python or Numba.
items.example: On 10 elements, `sum(x)` in Python and `np.sum(x)` are both negligible; profiling a 500k-element column shows the vectorized sum winning by a large margin.

items.myth: Broadcasting copies and tiles smaller arrays to match larger ones.
items.reality: Broadcasting is a shape-compatibility convention for element-wise ops; NumPy does not materialize a full tiled copy unless you explicitly call `np.tile` or `np.repeat`.
items.example: `X - mean` with `X` shape `(1000, 20)` and `mean` shape `(20,)` subtracts the same 20 means across rows without creating a `(1000, 20)` mean matrix in memory.

items.myth: Any NumPy function is automatically vectorized.
items.reality: True vectorization uses ufuncs and compiled loops. `np.vectorize` is a convenience wrapper that still calls Python once per element — it is not a performance fix.
items.example: `np.vectorize(lambda x: x * 2)(arr)` loops in Python; `arr * 2` uses a real vectorized ufunc.

items.myth: If code runs without error, broadcasting produced the correct geometry.
items.reality: Compatible shapes can still encode the wrong semantics — a row vector and column vector both broadcast, but they imply different grid layouts.
items.example: `a[:, None] + b[None, :]` builds an outer sum grid; `a + b` when both are 1-D adds element-wise only if lengths match, not an outer grid.
